EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level low pressure system moved out across the Plains during the evening of April 15th and the early morning hours of Tuesday April 16th. Storms developed ahead of an advancing cold front across Oklahoma into the Flint Hills of Kansas after midnight. Around 4AM one of the storms started to show low level rotation as it moved into southern Greenwood County and shortly after produced an EF-1 tornado southeast and east of Eureka. ||A few hours later, a line storms along the cold front moved into the Flint Hills with a brief but strong tornado occurring south of Virgil. This tornado was rated an EF-2 with damage including steel purlins being bent along with a large outbuilding ripped from its foundation.  Luckily there were no injuries of fatalities with these tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The damage survey revealed an EF2 tornado with an estimated peak wind speed of 118 mph occurred south of Virgil. There was damage to a farmstead to a large outbuilding that was ripped from its sturdy foundation and had bent steel purlins. Several large trees were snapped and uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and an associated cold front pushed through far southeast Kansas during the late evening hours on May 6, 2024. Thunderstorms developed along the front, growing upscale to a linear storm mode by the time they reached southeast Kansas. This line of thunderstorms produced a swath of damaging winds, along with two EF-0 tornadoes in Cherokee County before continuing into the Missouri Ozarks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media report of numerous trees down or uprooted. South side house gutters were damaged, with shingles blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around 2:30pm on Sunday May 19th, a high precipitation supercell developed near Hays along a warm front. This storm tracked east along I-70 producing a few brief tornadoes across Russell and northern Ellsworth county.  As it progressed east, the wind and hail threat ramp-up as it moved into Saline County.  The Salina Airport measured a 100 mph wind gust!  There was also numerous reports of golf ball size hail across Salina. ||A rapidly moving line of storms called a derecho then moved out of western Kansas and into Reno and Kingman counties shortly after 7 pm.  This intense line of storms produced widespread 70 to 90 mph winds as it raced off to the northeast at 70 to 80 mph. Hardest hit were the communities of Hutchinson Haven, Burrton, Halstead and Newton where widespread tree damage and power outages were reported.  Damage was also reported across southern Marion and northern Butler counties with  tornado damage was documented near Burns.  The destructive line of storms continued to produce damaging winds as it tracked toward Kansas City late Sunday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated to be around 60 mph. Powerlines were also reported to have been knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rich low level moisture rapidly lifted north across the Plains on Saturday May 25th with storms initially developing along a developing dryline across western Oklahoma. One particular storm gained strength as it tracked north of the Oklahoma/Kansas border. This supercell thunderstorm produced a brief tornado in Barber County before tracking east and producing an EF-2 tornado west of Anthony. What made this tornado unique was that it did not form from the main mesocyclone associated with the supercell, but rather from the supercells interaction with one of the storms lifting due north out of Oklahoma.||The tornado threat was short-lived though as storms lifting north out of northern Oklahoma merged with the supercell which transitioned the threat to damaging winds.  This cluster of storms produced 60 to 75 mph winds as it tracked across Wichita which caused widespread tree damage and widespread power outages.||Around midnight additional storms developed over southern Cowley County and rapidly starting rotating which required a tornado warning as it tracked toward the Kansas/Oklahoma border.  This storm went on to produce just shy of baseball size hail in Tyro and 80 mph winds in Caney.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail reports were shared via broadcast media and law enforcement which ranged from quarter to golf ball size near Caney. Wind gusts estimated to 80 mph were reported as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system that originated in the Plains brought an associated cold front through southeast Kansas during the late afternoon and evening hours on May 21, 2024. Discreet supercells quickly congealed into a linear mode, producing quarter size hail and damaging winds in Bourbon and Crawford counties before pushing into southwest Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media report relaying that many trees were snapped halfway, with some being uprooted in Fulton, Kansas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media report of hail to the size of quarters in Arma, Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dryline and Pacific cold front moved into the region on Monday May 6th, and with abundant moisture and instability already in place, storms erupted by the early afternoon hours across west-central Kansas. As storms tracked across central Kansas they produced hail up to slightly larger than baseballs! Storms became more numerous over southeast Kansas after sunset with wind and heavy rain becoming the main threats. The majority of the tornadoes on this day took place down in Oklahoma where an EF-4 struck the town of Barnsdall Oklahoma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty four hour rainfall from CoCoRaHS observer of 2.05 inches. Street flooding occurred in town during the late evening hours of the 6th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on Kansas Highway 99 occurred just south of US Highway 400 during the evening of the 6th. A mesonet site near Climax measured a twenty four hour rainfall total of 2.14 inches.

Primary damage indicators were residential houses with roof/siding damage, along with some businesses with roof/siding damage. The tornado dissipated|on the northeast side of Garden City by the high school.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe weather impacted Northwest Kansas as a strong low pressure system moved across the Central High Plains. Initially, two strong supercells moved over the southern halves of Logan and Gove counties producing up to grapefruit sized hail and two EF-Unknown landspout tornadoes. The hail and tornadoes primarily occurred in southern Gove County along and east of Highway 25. One of the landspouts developed along the Gove-Lane county line and continued on into northern Lane County. No damage was reported with either tornado. During the evening hours, a linear cluster of storms moved across Northwest Kansas from Colorado producing large hail and wind gusts estimated up to 96 mph. The strongest winds occurred in Eastern Graham County where estimated 90-96 mph straight line winds produced significant damage to two metal buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple baseball sized hailstones found on the property.

This cluster of storms expanded north and eastward continuing to produce quarter to two inch sized hail across south central and southeast Kansas. Meanwhile, a second cluster of storms developed across northwest Oklahoma and progressed into southern Kansas during the nighttime hours. While more sporadic, this cluster produced wind gusts to 60 mph and quarter to golf ball sized hail as it moved across south central and southeast Kansas early on the morning of May 16. A prolonged period of heavy rainfall caused some flooding across portions of Elk County as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe weather impacted Northwest Kansas as a strong low pressure system moved across the Central High Plains. Initially, two strong supercells moved over the southern halves of Logan and Gove counties producing up to grapefruit sized hail and two EF-Unknown landspout tornadoes. The hail and tornadoes primarily occurred in southern Gove County along and east of Highway 25. One of the landspouts developed along the Gove-Lane county line and continued on into northern Lane County. No damage was reported with either tornado. During the evening hours, a linear cluster of storms moved across Northwest Kansas from Colorado producing large hail and wind gusts estimated up to 96 mph. The strongest winds occurred in Eastern Graham County where estimated 90-96 mph straight line winds produced significant damage to two metal buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple storm chasers reported an EF-U landspout tornado in a field east of Highway 23. Location is an approximate and it did not appear to move far from where it developed over it's brief life. No damage was observed or reported.

A line of storms developed over Northwest Kansas, producing up to quarter sized hail. Majority of the area had at least a report of pea to half inch sized hail; however, Thomas, Logan, and Gove counties had hail the size of dime to quarters. The largest hail reported fell near the Monument Rocks in Logan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported on Highway 83.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around 2:30pm on Sunday May 19th, a high precipitation supercell developed near Hays along a warm front. This storm tracked east along I-70 producing a few brief tornadoes across Russell and northern Ellsworth county.  As it progressed east, the wind and hail threat ramp-up as it moved into Saline County.  The Salina Airport measured a 100 mph wind gust!  There was also numerous reports of golf ball size hail across Salina. ||A rapidly moving line of storms called a derecho then moved out of western Kansas and into Reno and Kingman counties shortly after 7 pm.  This intense line of storms produced widespread 70 to 90 mph winds as it raced off to the northeast at 70 to 80 mph. Hardest hit were the communities of Hutchinson Haven, Burrton, Halstead and Newton where widespread tree damage and power outages were reported.  Damage was also reported across southern Marion and northern Butler counties with  tornado damage was documented near Burns.  The destructive line of storms continued to produce damaging winds as it tracked toward Kansas City late Sunday night.

A low pressure system entered northwestern Kansas on the 6th and quickly moved east. There was a steep pressure rise as the low was leaving the area through the 7th. This sharp change in pressure caused winds to gust up to 72 MPH at Goodland. These winds kicked up blowing dust, creating brownout conditions and causing vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two mesonet sites reported wind gusts over 64 MPH over a 15 minute period. One location, south-southeast of Norton reported a 67 MPH gust. Impacts were high winds causing hazardous driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours, scattered storms developed over Northwest Kansas and moved north-northeast. These storms produced mainly hail that ranged in size from pea to hen egg sized, with the largest occurring near Grinnell in northern Gove County. Most gusts associated with these storms were sub-severe; however, there was an isolated 61 mph gust near Norton, Kansas. A lightning strike started an oil tank battery on fire in southern Logan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike started an oil tank battery on fire in southern Logan County. The fire burned for about an hour before being extinguished.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and storms formed east of the Front Range during the mid to late afternoon hours on May 25th. These thunderstorms then ejected outflow across the area leading to some reports of winds in excess of 55 mph. Further east into the late afternoon severe storms developed along a dry line, creating a few instances of severe hail up to half dollar size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated pea to penny, but mostly dime sized hail in Bogue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dryline and Pacific cold front moved into the region on Monday May 6th, and with abundant moisture and instability already in place, storms erupted by the early afternoon hours across west-central Kansas. As storms tracked across central Kansas they produced hail up to slightly larger than baseballs! Storms became more numerous over southeast Kansas after sunset with wind and heavy rain becoming the main threats. The majority of the tornadoes on this day took place down in Oklahoma where an EF-4 struck the town of Barnsdall Oklahoma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report relayed via the media of a tornado near the state line and south of Hewins. No damage was reported in that area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado tracked northwest and damaged a farmstead west of Anthony. What made this tornado unique was that it did not form from the main mesocyclone associated with the supercell, but rather from the supercell's interaction with one of the storms lifting due north out of Oklahoma. EF-2 damage was found at a farm about 8 miles west of Anthony where large sections of a roof structure were removed. A couple tree trunks were snapped with another one uprooted. One outbuilding was totally destroyed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two reports of blowing dust with an estimated visibility less than 0.25 mile in blowing dust within five miles of St. Francis. Impacts include health and driving hazards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours, scattered storms developed over Northwest Kansas and moved north-northeast. These storms produced mainly hail that ranged in size from pea to hen egg sized, with the largest occurring near Grinnell in northern Gove County. Most gusts associated with these storms were sub-severe; however, there was an isolated 61 mph gust near Norton, Kansas. A lightning strike started an oil tank battery on fire in southern Logan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranged in size from one to two inches in diameter and fell for a duration of two to three minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms developed over Northwest Kansas, producing up to quarter sized hail. Majority of the area had at least a report of pea to half inch sized hail; however, Thomas, Logan, and Gove counties had hail the size of dime to quarters. The largest hail reported fell near the Monument Rocks in Logan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to nickel sized hail observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around 2:30pm on Sunday May 19th, a high precipitation supercell developed near Hays along a warm front. This storm tracked east along I-70 producing a few brief tornadoes across Russell and northern Ellsworth county.  As it progressed east, the wind and hail threat ramp-up as it moved into Saline County.  The Salina Airport measured a 100 mph wind gust!  There was also numerous reports of golf ball size hail across Salina. ||A rapidly moving line of storms called a derecho then moved out of western Kansas and into Reno and Kingman counties shortly after 7 pm.  This intense line of storms produced widespread 70 to 90 mph winds as it raced off to the northeast at 70 to 80 mph. Hardest hit were the communities of Hutchinson Haven, Burrton, Halstead and Newton where widespread tree damage and power outages were reported.  Damage was also reported across southern Marion and northern Butler counties with  tornado damage was documented near Burns.  The destructive line of storms continued to produce damaging winds as it tracked toward Kansas City late Sunday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Off duty NWS employee had a personal weather station measure a 72 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low pressure system exited the High Plains, high winds developed on the back side of the low with winds out of the northwest. Sporadic gusts up to 64 mph were reported across several counties in Northwest Kansas, with the peak gusts occurring in Sherman and Wallace counties. These strong winds also created a dust storm that impacted the area with patchy to, at times, isolated visibility reductions of one-quarter mile and less in Sherman, Thomas, Wallace, and Greeley counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a duration of approximately one hour and fifteen minutes sporadic gusts of 58 to 62 mph occurred across Wichita County. The peak gust of 62 mph was recorded via a mesonet station near the Greeley County line in the northwest corner of the county. Key impacts: hazardous travel and high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and storms formed east of the Front Range during the mid to late afternoon hours on May 25th. These thunderstorms then ejected outflow across the area leading to some reports of winds in excess of 55 mph. Further east into the late afternoon severe storms developed along a dry line, creating a few instances of severe hail up to half dollar size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread 60+ mph winds occurred across Sherman county from an ESE moving outflow boundary as an off duty NWS employee estimated 60-65 mph winds 10 miles to the north of Edson. Additional measured 60 mph winds were recorded 10 miles north of Goodland and at the ASOS at the Goodland Airport. A power pole was also downed in the town of Goodland; albeit the bottom of the pole was reported to be in poor shape.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter mile visibility occurred with blowing dust which lasted for less than 5 minutes. AWOS observations in Oakley and Colby which are about 12 miles from the location of the blowing dust both reported wind gusts in the 35-40 mph range during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dryline and Pacific cold front moved into the region on Monday May 6th, and with abundant moisture and instability already in place, storms erupted by the early afternoon hours across west-central Kansas. As storms tracked across central Kansas they produced hail up to slightly larger than baseballs! Storms became more numerous over southeast Kansas after sunset with wind and heavy rain becoming the main threats. The majority of the tornadoes on this day took place down in Oklahoma where an EF-4 struck the town of Barnsdall Oklahoma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms that previously had produced 70-85 mph across portions of north-central Kansas in WFO Hastings' area weakened as they moved into Republic and Cloud Counties early morning on May 2. One 59 mph gust was measured in Republic County, as well as an area of 1 to 2 inches of rain across north-central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured 59 mph gust at personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another severe weather episode pounded northeast Kansas during the evening of May 19th.  At least 2 small tornadoes also occurred with more widespread damaging winds and large hail reports also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Survey revealed tree and tree limb damage in a field.  The tornado may have developed just northwest of a structure where some wind damage was noted however this location appeared to have been east of the actual circulation so it may have been enhanced downdraft winds on the east flank of the tornado that did that minor damage.  Radar also showed a CC minimum or a debris signature with this circulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another severe weather episode pounded northeast Kansas during the evening of May 19th.  At least 2 small tornadoes also occurred with more widespread damaging winds and large hail reports also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Survey revealed a small brief tornado in this location.  Tree limb damage was noted to live oaks and white pines.  Radar data did also show a small debris signature (CC minimum) in this location with the velocity rotation signature.  There was no access to the east.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe weather impacted Northwest Kansas as a strong low pressure system moved across the Central High Plains. Initially, two strong supercells moved over the southern halves of Logan and Gove counties producing up to grapefruit sized hail and two EF-Unknown landspout tornadoes. The hail and tornadoes primarily occurred in southern Gove County along and east of Highway 25. One of the landspouts developed along the Gove-Lane county line and continued on into northern Lane County. No damage was reported with either tornado. During the evening hours, a linear cluster of storms moved across Northwest Kansas from Colorado producing large hail and wind gusts estimated up to 96 mph. The strongest winds occurred in Eastern Graham County where estimated 90-96 mph straight line winds produced significant damage to two metal buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to baseball sized hail reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours, scattered storms developed over Northwest Kansas and moved north-northeast. These storms produced mainly hail that ranged in size from pea to hen egg sized, with the largest occurring near Grinnell in northern Gove County. Most gusts associated with these storms were sub-severe; however, there was an isolated 61 mph gust near Norton, Kansas. A lightning strike started an oil tank battery on fire in southern Logan County.
Thunderstorms producing large hail occurred across Kansas during the late morning.  The hail was up to golf ball in size.
Thunderstorms producing large hail occurred across Kansas during the late morning.  The hail was up to golf ball in size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to golf ball size occurred.

Around 2:30pm on Sunday May 19th, a high precipitation supercell developed near Hays along a warm front. Hardest hit were the communities of Hutchinson Haven, Burrton, Halstead and Newton where widespread tree damage and power outages were reported.  Damage was also reported across southern Marion and northern Butler counties with  tornado damage was documented near Burns.  The destructive line of storms continued to produce damaging winds as it tracked toward Kansas City late Sunday night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rich low level moisture rapidly lifted north across the Plains on Saturday May 25th with storms initially developing along a developing dryline across western Oklahoma. One particular storm gained strength as it tracked north of the Oklahoma/Kansas border. This supercell thunderstorm produced a brief tornado in Barber County before tracking east and producing an EF-2 tornado west of Anthony. What made this tornado unique was that it did not form from the main mesocyclone associated with the supercell, but rather from the supercells interaction with one of the storms lifting due north out of Oklahoma.||The tornado threat was short-lived though as storms lifting north out of northern Oklahoma merged with the supercell which transitioned the threat to damaging winds.  This cluster of storms produced 60 to 75 mph winds as it tracked across Wichita which caused widespread tree damage and widespread power outages.||Around midnight additional storms developed over southern Cowley County and rapidly starting rotating which required a tornado warning as it tracked toward the Kansas/Oklahoma border.  This storm went on to produce just shy of baseball size hail in Tyro and 80 mph winds in Caney.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Caney Police Department estimated 80 mph winds and had quarter sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low pressure system exited the High Plains, high winds developed on the back side of the low with winds out of the northwest. Sporadic gusts up to 64 mph were reported across several counties in Northwest Kansas, with the peak gusts occurring in Sherman and Wallace counties. These strong winds also created a dust storm that impacted the area with patchy to, at times, isolated visibility reductions of one-quarter mile and less in Sherman, Thomas, Wallace, and Greeley counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a duration of approximately two hours sporadic gusts of 58 to 61 mph occurred across Thomas County. The peak gust of 61 mph was recorded via a mesonet station in Rexford. Key impacts: hazardous travel, high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low pressure system exited the High Plains, high winds developed on the back side of the low with winds out of the northwest. These strong winds also created a dust storm that impacted the area with patchy to, at times, isolated visibility reductions of one-quarter mile and less in Sherman, Thomas, Wallace, and Greeley counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a roughly two hour duration blowing dust occurred due to high winds gusting up to 61 mph. Visibility was reported to be lowest with intermittent brown out conditions near Brewster between mile markers 39 and 39 on I-70. Key impacts: hazardous travel, brown out conditions, dust storm, high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system entered northwestern Kansas on the 6th and quickly moved east. There was a steep pressure rise as the low was leaving the area through the 7th. This sharp change in pressure caused winds to gust up to 72 MPH at Goodland. These winds kicked up blowing dust, creating brownout conditions and causing vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported brown out conditions along Highway 83, causing a multiple vehicle accident around CR M and Hwy 83. No injuries or fatalities were reported. Impacts include health and driving hazards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low pressure system exited the High Plains, high winds developed on the back side of the low with winds out of the northwest. Sporadic gusts up to 64 mph were reported across several counties in Northwest Kansas, with the peak gusts occurring in Sherman and Wallace counties. These strong winds also created a dust storm that impacted the area with patchy to, at times, isolated visibility reductions of one-quarter mile and less in Sherman, Thomas, Wallace, and Greeley counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a duration of approximately thirty-five minutes sporadic gusts up to 58 mph occurred across Greeley County. The peak gust of 58 mph was recorded via a mesonet station six miles east of Tribune. While the high winds were occurring, blowing dust was observed north of Tribune with visibility going down to 100 feet at least once, but averaging around one mile.  The lowest visibility was next to a feed yard where dust was blowing off the roads and parking area. No other dust or visibility reports have been found. Key impacts: hazardous travel, high winds, blowing dust, and visibility reductions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into central Kansas during the afternoon hours of April 30th and storms erupted along it by around 5 pm. Extreme instability allowed storms to quickly become severe producing large hail and a landspout tornado.  Storms continued to intensify as they tracked south and east with one of the storms producing up to baseball sized hail in Harper County! In addition, before moving into Oklahoma, one storm started to show impressive low level rotation and eventually produced a brief EF-1 tornado north of Sedan. One brief EF-U tornado occurred near Augusta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration severe weather event occurred late afternoon into the night of May 6th.  Supercell thunderstorms tracked across parts of central and northeast Kansas producing an EF0 tornado on the south side of Manhattan Kansas with baseball size hail also occurring with one of the storms.   Additional storms developed after sunset across southeast Kansas and produced wind damage and also another small tornado south of Burlington Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage survey revealed an overturned irrigation pivot and a narrow area of substantial tree damage along a short path northeast before it appeared to have lifted near the Kansas river.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager provided photos of damage that radar suggests could have been associated with a small tornado embedded with a developing line of storms.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Survey revealed an old barn that had its metal roof panels removed and tossed approximately one half mile to the northeast into open fields.  A narrow path of tree limb damage was also found around 1 mile to the southwest of the barn.  The damage was suggestive of a small brief tornado in this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low pressure system exited the High Plains, high winds developed on the back side of the low with winds out of the northwest. Sporadic gusts up to 64 mph were reported across several counties in Northwest Kansas, with the peak gusts occurring in Sherman and Wallace counties. These strong winds also created a dust storm that impacted the area with patchy to, at times, isolated visibility reductions of one-quarter mile and less in Sherman, Thomas, Wallace, and Greeley counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An isolated 59 mph high wind gust occurred over the Hill City Airport and was recorded via the ASOS station there. No other high wind gusts of 58 mph or higher were observed or reported across the remainder of the county. Key Impacts: Hazardous travel, high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low pressure system exited the High Plains, high winds developed on the back side of the low with winds out of the northwest. Sporadic gusts up to 64 mph were reported across several counties in Northwest Kansas, with the peak gusts occurring in Sherman and Wallace counties. These strong winds also created a dust storm that impacted the area with patchy to, at times, isolated visibility reductions of one-quarter mile and less in Sherman, Thomas, Wallace, and Greeley counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a duration of approximately one hour and forty-five minutes sporadic gusts of 59 to 64 mph occurred across Wallace County. The peak gust of 64 mph was recorded via a mesonet station near Sharon Springs. Key impacts: hazardous travel and high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around 2:30pm on Sunday May 19th, a high precipitation supercell developed near Hays along a warm front. This storm tracked east along I-70 producing a few brief tornadoes across Russell and northern Ellsworth county.  As it progressed east, the wind and hail threat ramp-up as it moved into Saline County.  The Salina Airport measured a 100 mph wind gust!  There was also numerous reports of golf ball size hail across Salina. ||A rapidly moving line of storms called a derecho then moved out of western Kansas and into Reno and Kingman counties shortly after 7 pm.  This intense line of storms produced widespread 70 to 90 mph winds as it raced off to the northeast at 70 to 80 mph. Hardest hit were the communities of Hutchinson Haven, Burrton, Halstead and Newton where widespread tree damage and power outages were reported.  Damage was also reported across southern Marion and northern Butler counties with  tornado damage was documented near Burns.  The destructive line of storms continued to produce damaging winds as it tracked toward Kansas City late Sunday night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent and efficient rainfall across parts of east-central Kansas resulted in 3 to 5 inch rainfall totals and isolated flooding in Anderson County. Occurred late evening on the 30th into the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads remained closed south of Garnett as of 1:48 AM cdt. Campground near cedar creek reservoir was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours, scattered storms developed over Northwest Kansas and moved north-northeast. These storms produced mainly hail that ranged in size from pea to hen egg sized, with the largest occurring near Grinnell in northern Gove County. Most gusts associated with these storms were sub-severe; however, there was an isolated 61 mph gust near Norton, Kansas. A lightning strike started an oil tank battery on fire in southern Logan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized accumulated on the ground near Grainfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system entered northwestern Kansas on the 6th and quickly moved east. There was a steep pressure rise as the low was leaving the area through the 7th. This sharp change in pressure caused winds to gust up to 72 MPH at Goodland. These winds kicked up blowing dust, creating brownout conditions and causing vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager's weather station recorded a 62 MPH gust at 11:45 AM CST and the St. Francis AWOS recorded multiple gusts up to 59 MPH through 3:15 PM CST. Impacts were high winds causing blowing dust and hazardous driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system entered northwestern Kansas on the 6th and quickly moved east. There was a steep pressure rise as the low was leaving the area through the 7th. This sharp change in pressure caused winds to gust up to 72 MPH at Goodland. These winds kicked up blowing dust, creating brownout conditions and causing vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oberlin AWOS recorded multiple gusts over 58 MPH through 12:35 PM CST starting at 4:15 AM CST. The winds did briefly lower in the morning around sunrise, but returned to over 58 MPH. Impacts were high winds causing hazardous driving conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A WeatherLink site reported a gust of 61 MPH. This is the only evidence of winds in excess of 58 MPH in Gove county. A report near Campus was sent in of small branches and a few shingles were blown down. Impacts were high winds causing hazardous driving conditions and mild property damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed along an eastward advancing dry line. Large hail was the primary severe hazard with this activity, with several instances of nickel to half dollar size hail occurring across northwest Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Videos of hail show a copious amount of hail falling to turn the ground white. Trained spotter reported that the largest hail stone was around 1.25 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Saturday April 27th, there was volatile severe weather across Kansas and Oklahoma. An abundance of moisture and increasing shear through the day resulted in numerous rotating storms which produced several tornadoes across the Flint Hills into southeast Kansas. The strongest tornado was an EF-2 which occurred in Cowley County which did damage to a few outbuildings. Luckily there were no injuries or fatalities reported with these storms. There are 12 confirmed tornadoes from this event with most of them being EF-U or EF-0. These storms did produce hail ranging from quarter to golf ball size and some damaging wind gusts. ||Flooding eventually became a major issue as storms trained across southeast Kansas for several hours into the wee hours of the 28th. Between April 25th and April 28th, many locations across southeast Kansas picked up between 7 and 10 inches of rainfall which sent the Neosho, Verdigris and Fall rivers into flood. Five forecast points rose to moderate flood stage. There was damage to several county roads from the flooding with complete washouts, and part of a railroad track in Wilson County was taken out from water eroding the embankment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer measured a total of 7.28 inches in the last 24 hours. Additional rainfall reports across the county ranged from 5.47 inches in Iola to 6 inches in Humboldt. Some of the typical roadways flooded in the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees limbs around four inches in diameter were downed in Humboldt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rich low level moisture rapidly lifted north across the Plains on Saturday May 25th with storms initially developing along a developing dryline across western Oklahoma. One particular storm gained strength as it tracked north of the Oklahoma/Kansas border. This supercell thunderstorm produced a brief tornado in Barber County before tracking east and producing an EF-2 tornado west of Anthony. What made this tornado unique was that it did not form from the main mesocyclone associated with the supercell, but rather from the supercells interaction with one of the storms lifting due north out of Oklahoma.||The tornado threat was short-lived though as storms lifting north out of northern Oklahoma merged with the supercell which transitioned the threat to damaging winds.  This cluster of storms produced 60 to 75 mph winds as it tracked across Wichita which caused widespread tree damage and widespread power outages.||Around midnight additional storms developed over southern Cowley County and rapidly starting rotating which required a tornado warning as it tracked toward the Kansas/Oklahoma border.  This storm went on to produce just shy of baseball size hail in Tyro and 80 mph winds in Caney.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strother Field Airport measured wind gusts of 60 to 74 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Saturday April 27th, there was volatile severe weather across Kansas and Oklahoma. An abundance of moisture and increasing shear through the day resulted in numerous rotating storms which produced several tornadoes across the Flint Hills into southeast Kansas. The strongest tornado was an EF-2 which occurred in Cowley County which did damage to a few outbuildings. Luckily there were no injuries or fatalities reported with these storms. There are 12 confirmed tornadoes from this event with most of them being EF-U or EF-0. These storms did produce hail ranging from quarter to golf ball size and some damaging wind gusts. ||Flooding eventually became a major issue as storms trained across southeast Kansas for several hours into the wee hours of the 28th. Between April 25th and April 28th, many locations across southeast Kansas picked up between 7 and 10 inches of rainfall which sent the Neosho, Verdigris and Fall rivers into flood. Five forecast points rose to moderate flood stage. There was damage to several county roads from the flooding with complete washouts, and part of a railroad track in Wilson County was taken out from water eroding the embankment.

A strong cold front moved across Northwest Kansas producing high winds along and south of I-70 in addition to a line of thunderstorms that moved along the Nebraska state line. Both the high winds and the thunderstorm winds produced measured gusts of 58-60 mph, with the peak estimated gust of 100 mph based on a snapped power pole west of Atwood as a thunderstorm moved over the location.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a duration of 25 minutes, high winds from behind a cold front produced up to 58 mph gusts near the town of Winona, Kansas. No other high wind criteria gusts were observed across Logan County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe weather impacted Northwest Kansas as a strong low pressure system moved across the Central High Plains. Initially, two strong supercells moved over the southern halves of Logan and Gove counties producing up to grapefruit sized hail and two EF-Unknown landspout tornadoes. The hail and tornadoes primarily occurred in southern Gove County along and east of Highway 25. One of the landspouts developed along the Gove-Lane county line and continued on into northern Lane County. No damage was reported with either tornado. During the evening hours, a linear cluster of storms moved across Northwest Kansas from Colorado producing large hail and wind gusts estimated up to 96 mph. The strongest winds occurred in Eastern Graham County where estimated 90-96 mph straight line winds produced significant damage to two metal buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Metal garage had overhead doors blown out and folded with portions of the side wall torn away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around 2:30pm on Sunday May 19th, a high precipitation supercell developed near Hays along a warm front. This storm tracked east along I-70 producing a few brief tornadoes across Russell and northern Ellsworth county.  As it progressed east, the wind and hail threat ramp-up as it moved into Saline County.  The Salina Airport measured a 100 mph wind gust!  There was also numerous reports of golf ball size hail across Salina. ||A rapidly moving line of storms called a derecho then moved out of western Kansas and into Reno and Kingman counties shortly after 7 pm.  This intense line of storms produced widespread 70 to 90 mph winds as it raced off to the northeast at 70 to 80 mph. Hardest hit were the communities of Hutchinson Haven, Burrton, Halstead and Newton where widespread tree damage and power outages were reported.  Damage was also reported across southern Marion and northern Butler counties with  tornado damage was documented near Burns.  The destructive line of storms continued to produce damaging winds as it tracked toward Kansas City late Sunday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust was measured at 70 mph. At least four inch diameter tree limbs were downed.

The most severe damage was on the west side of town to an outbuilding which had collapsed doors ultimately leading to some damage to the roof and wall of the structure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed during the afternoon hours over the Flint Hills on Friday, April 26th along an eastward moving dryline. As the storms progressed east they started to produce five tornadoes. Two of the tornadoes were rated an EF-2. One of them struck southwest of Fredonia and the other southwest of Yates Center. Luckily there were no injuries or fatalities with these tornadoes. The other tornadoes were rated EF1, EF0 and EFU. Large hail ranging from quarter size to ping pong ball size was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Saturday April 27th, there was volatile severe weather across Kansas and Oklahoma. An abundance of moisture and increasing shear through the day resulted in numerous rotating storms which produced several tornadoes across the Flint Hills into southeast Kansas. The strongest tornado was an EF-2 which occurred in Cowley County which did damage to a few outbuildings. Luckily there were no injuries or fatalities reported with these storms. There are 12 confirmed tornadoes from this event with most of them being EF-U or EF-0. These storms did produce hail ranging from quarter to golf ball size and some damaging wind gusts. ||Flooding eventually became a major issue as storms trained across southeast Kansas for several hours into the wee hours of the 28th. Between April 25th and April 28th, many locations across southeast Kansas picked up between 7 and 10 inches of rainfall which sent the Neosho, Verdigris and Fall rivers into flood. Five forecast points rose to moderate flood stage. There was damage to several county roads from the flooding with complete washouts, and part of a railroad track in Wilson County was taken out from water eroding the embankment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two photos of the tornado were shared via social media. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and an associated cold front pushed through far southeast Kansas during the late evening hours on May 6, 2024. Thunderstorms developed along the front, growing upscale to a linear storm mode by the time they reached southeast Kansas. This line of thunderstorms produced a swath of damaging winds, along with two EF-0 tornadoes in Cherokee County before continuing into the Missouri Ozarks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS survey team determined that a tornado touched down to the northwest of Columbus near the intersection of NW Center Star and NW 40th St, downing trees in a convergent to rotational manner as it tracked northeast.  As the tornado neared NW 30th St, several outbuildings were damaged to destroyed, with another barn on NW Lawton Rd experiencing door damage on its southeast and east sides. The tornado then destroyed one barn and damaged another as it crossed NW Belleview Road before dissipating.

Between April 25th and April 28th, many locations across southeast Kansas picked up between 7 and 10 inches of rainfall which sent the Neosho, Verdigris and Fall rivers into flood. Five forecast points rose to moderate flood stage. There was damage to several county roads from the flooding with complete washouts, and part of a railroad track in Wilson County was taken out from water eroding the embankment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranged from quarter to golf ball sized hail in Sycamore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across Northwest Kansas producing high winds along and south of I-70 in addition to a line of thunderstorms that moved along the Nebraska state line. Both the high winds and the thunderstorm winds produced measured gusts of 58-60 mph, with the peak estimated gust of 100 mph based on a snapped power pole west of Atwood as a thunderstorm moved over the location.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds associated with a cold front over Sherman County produced 45 to 59 mph gusts. The strongest gust of 59 mph occurred at the Goodland Airport and was the only observation to hit High Wind Warning criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms rapidly developed over northern Oklahoma during the late afternoon hours on Tuesday July 2nd and quickly tracked off to the northeast. These storms quickly started producing damaging straight-line winds as they moved into Harper and Kingman counties with gusts of 60 to 65 mph reported.||Line of severe storms moved across Wichita between 5 and 6 pm and produced widespread wind damage with numerous trees reported blown down. Winds across the city were measured between 60 and 70 mph. As the storms moved into Butler County, strong winds blew down a fireworks tent which resulted in three injuries. Damaging winds impacted other portions of south central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported powerlines were down and relayed to emergency management.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms tracked out of northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska during the early evening hours of Wednesday July 3rd and moved into central Kansas around 11 pm. The line of storms accelerated southeast and produced a large swath of damaging winds as they tracked southeast through the overnight hours.||There were multiple reports of 70 mph winds with the Salina Airport recording an 82 mph wind gust with a mesonet in McPherson county reporting a 76 mph gust. Unfortunately there were 3 injuries and one fatality on Wilson Lake in Russell county when a number of campers were rolled by the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported an estimated gust of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cluster of storms migrated out of western Kansas during the early morning hours of July 16th and strengthened as they approached north central Kansas. Storms produced intense straight-line winds as they tracked along I-70 with multiple reports of 70 to 90 mph winds. A Kansas Mesonet site north of Bunker Hill reported a 93 mph wind gust! Power poles were snapped in Russell County along with a couple campers rolled over near at Wilson Lake campgrounds. The campers were thankfully unoccupied.

Unfortunately there were 3 injuries and one fatality on Wilson Lake in Russell county when a number of campers were rolled by the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported an estimated 60 mph wind gust with an eight inch tree limb down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cluster of storms migrated out of western Kansas during the early morning hours of July 16th and strengthened as they approached north central Kansas. Storms produced intense straight-line winds as they tracked along I-70 with multiple reports of 70 to 90 mph winds. A Kansas Mesonet site north of Bunker Hill reported a 93 mph wind gust! Power poles were snapped in Russell County along with a couple campers rolled over near at Wilson Lake campgrounds. The campers were thankfully unoccupied.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 93 mph wind gust was recorded by the Kansas State Mesonet near Bunker Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers and thunderstorms moved across parts of Montgomery, Chautauqua, Elk and Labette counties during the evening of April 1st. A severe thunderstorm watch was issued for the affect region and a number of severe thunderstorm warnings were issued. Winds of 70 mph, hail up to 2.75 inches (baseballs) and numerous reports of thunderstorm damage were reported from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours, scattered storms developed over Northwest Kansas and moved north-northeast. These storms produced mainly hail that ranged in size from pea to hen egg sized, with the largest occurring near Grinnell in northern Gove County. Most gusts associated with these storms were sub-severe; however, there was an isolated 61 mph gust near Norton, Kansas. A lightning strike started an oil tank battery on fire in southern Logan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to around quarter sized hail fell over a duration of seven minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Line of storms ahead of a strong low pressure system produced a concentrated swath of 70-90 mph winds in northeast Kansas up to the Nebraska border, with just a few isolated wind gust reports elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several outhouse buildings and power poles damaged. One house was destroyed as it slid off its foundation and one occupant inside it was injured. Estimated 80-90 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the late afternoon of the 30th, storms began to fire across eastern Colorado and moved northeast through northwestern Kansas. Later in the night, another round of storms moved east through the area. These rounds of storm produced heavy, prolonged rain in the Colby area, causing minor urban flooding to occur. Between the two rounds of storms, a wake low produced a single gust of 61 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into central Kansas during the afternoon hours of April 30th and storms erupted along it by around 5 pm. Extreme instability allowed storms to quickly become severe producing large hail and a landspout tornado.  Storms continued to intensify as they tracked south and east with one of the storms producing up to baseball sized hail in Harper County! In addition, before moving into Oklahoma, one storm started to show impressive low level rotation and eventually produced a brief EF-1 tornado north of Sedan. One brief EF-U tornado occurred near Augusta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from a trained spotter of winds up to 70 mph. Tree limbs and a couple trees were knocked down as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions in eastern Kansas improved significantly with no areas of severe drought (D2) remaining.  In central and parts of south central Kansas, drought conditions worsened with much of central Kansas now in the severe drought (D2) category.  This was due to the vast majority of the rain falling in southeast Kansas during the month of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened across the county from moderate drought (D1) to severe drought (D2).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across western Kansas during the afternoon hours which led to dime to nickel size hail in Sharon Springs along with a 63 mph wind gust in Sherman county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WeatherLink site Rietcheck Farms recorded the wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of NW Kansas during the late morning through the afternoon hours. Hail up to golf ball size was reported in Logan county, along with 60-70 mph winds reported in Norton, Decatur, and Thomas counties.

Thunderstorms developed across portions of NW Kansas during the late morning through the afternoon hours. Hail up to golf ball size was reported in Logan county, along with 60-70 mph winds reported in Norton, Decatur, and Thomas counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours a line of scattered storms developed over Northwest Kansas producing estimated gusts up to 90 mph, primarily in Thomas County, with damage to a shed, vehicle windows, and trees in the city of Colby. Additional wind gusts of 58 mph were reported near Winona in Logan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An isolated high wind gust of 58 mph occurred ahead of thunderstorm outflow. Observation was recorded via Weather Underground site KKSMARIE11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning around 7 pm, severe Thunderstorms spread into the area with a mix of impacts ranging from quarter to ping pong ball sized hail and severe wind gusts, reported as high as 70 mph, as was reported by the Garden City Airport observation at 9:20 pm. A torrential rain environment was also in place, as street flooding quickly developed in the town of Scott City. The severe weather continued into the overnight and early morning hours of July 7tt,  as 67 mph winds were measured by the mesonet station near Copeland around 2 AM. A prolific outflow boundary developed, moving across Gray county. A Damage survey conducted yielded estimated 100 mph wind gusts 5 miles southwest of Charleston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS station at Garden City Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms tracked out of northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska during the early evening hours of Wednesday July 3rd and moved into central Kansas around 11 pm. The line of storms accelerated southeast and produced a large swath of damaging winds as they tracked southeast through the overnight hours.||There were multiple reports of 70 mph winds with the Salina Airport recording an 82 mph wind gust with a mesonet in McPherson county reporting a 76 mph gust. Unfortunately there were 3 injuries and one fatality on Wilson Lake in Russell county when a number of campers were rolled by the strong winds.

Around 7 pm, discreet thunderstorms were moving south of Interstate 70 into the smoky hill region, while a more widespread severe line of storms was moving out of Colorado into the far southwest counties. Severe Thunderstorm Watches (#550 and 551) were in effect for the area as the line developed a large bow echo moving east across of most of the area south of highway 400. Numerous severe wind gusts were reported or recorded by spotters and mesonets, including two half dollar size hail reports in the Ford and Spearville areas of Ford county from a supercell that formed ahead of the convective line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms wind gusts were measured by a mesonet station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of high wind occurred over much of Kansas during the 6th and continued through the 7th.  There were numerous reports of non-thunderstorm winds of 50 mph to nearly 70 mph in many areas of central Kansas. A high wind warning was in effect at the time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 58 mph was recorded at the Russell Municipal Airport during the mid morning with gusts of 50 to 55 mph into the early afternoon.

Storms developed during the afternoon hours over the Flint Hills on Friday, April 26th along an eastward moving dryline. As the storms progressed east they started to produce five tornadoes. Two of the tornadoes were rated an EF-2. One of them struck southwest of Fredonia and the other southwest of Yates Center. Luckily there were no injuries or fatalities with these tornadoes. The other tornadoes were rated EF1, EF0 and EFU. Large hail ranging from quarter size to ping pong ball size was reported.

A cold front moved southward across Kansas into a hot and humid airmass during the evening hours on the 31st. This sparked off a line of strong to severe thunderstorms which produced damaging to destructive winds across the northern and central Kansas. The Salina area had extensive tree damage and power outages from thunderstorm wind gusts over 80mph with a measurement of 95 mph in downtown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kansas State Mesonet recorded a wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours a line of scattered storms developed over Northwest Kansas producing estimated gusts up to 90 mph, primarily in Thomas County, with damage to a shed, vehicle windows, and trees in the city of Colby. Additional wind gusts of 58 mph were reported near Winona in Logan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tin shed that was unanchored was blown apart with one tree uprooted, several tree limbs down, and a portion of a rear windshield blown out of a truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning around 7 pm, severe Thunderstorms spread into the area with a mix of impacts ranging from quarter to ping pong ball sized hail and severe wind gusts, reported as high as 70 mph, as was reported by the Garden City Airport observation at 9:20 pm. A torrential rain environment was also in place, as street flooding quickly developed in the town of Scott City. The severe weather continued into the overnight and early morning hours of July 7tt,  as 67 mph winds were measured by the mesonet station near Copeland around 2 AM. A prolific outflow boundary developed, moving across Gray county. A Damage survey conducted yielded estimated 100 mph wind gusts 5 miles southwest of Charleston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station UP856 0.8 E Yocemento (UPR).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions in eastern Kansas improved significantly with no areas of severe drought (D2) remaining.  In central and parts of south central Kansas, drought conditions worsened with much of central Kansas now in the severe drought (D2) category.  This was due to the vast majority of the rain falling in southeast Kansas during the month of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened slightly in the northwest parts of the county with the area of severe drought (D2) expanding some. The remainder of the county remained in moderate drought (D1).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms tracked out of northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska during the early evening hours of Wednesday July 3rd and moved into central Kansas around 11 pm. The line of storms accelerated southeast and produced a large swath of damaging winds as they tracked southeast through the overnight hours.||There were multiple reports of 70 mph winds with the Salina Airport recording an 82 mph wind gust with a mesonet in McPherson county reporting a 76 mph gust. Unfortunately there were 3 injuries and one fatality on Wilson Lake in Russell county when a number of campers were rolled by the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported an estimated wind speed of 70 mph.

Around 7 pm, discreet thunderstorms were moving south of Interstate 70 into the smoky hill region, while a more widespread severe line of storms was moving out of Colorado into the far southwest counties. Severe Thunderstorm Watches (#550 and 551) were in effect for the area as the line developed a large bow echo moving east across of most of the area south of highway 400. Numerous severe wind gusts were reported or recorded by spotters and mesonets, including two half dollar size hail reports in the Ford and Spearville areas of Ford county from a supercell that formed ahead of the convective line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured by a mesonet station.

A cold front moved into central Kansas during the afternoon hours of April 30th and storms erupted along it by around 5 pm. Extreme instability allowed storms to quickly become severe producing large hail and a landspout tornado.  Storms continued to intensify as they tracked south and east with one of the storms producing up to baseball sized hail in Harper County! In addition, before moving into Oklahoma, one storm started to show impressive low level rotation and eventually produced a brief EF-1 tornado north of Sedan. One brief EF-U tornado occurred near Augusta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An irrigation pivot below onto the roadway at 60th and Emma Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of showers and thunderstorms moved across parts of Montgomery, Chautauqua, Elk and Labette counties during the evening of April 1st. A severe thunderstorm watch was issued for the affect region and a number of severe thunderstorm warnings were issued. Winds of 70 mph, hail up to 2.75 inches (baseballs) and numerous reports of thunderstorm damage were reported from this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Windows broken by the combination of large hail and strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours of April 27th, severe storms impacted northeast and eastern Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media report of trees and large limbs downed in Pleasanton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved southward across Kansas into a hot and humid airmass during the evening hours on the 31st. This sparked off a line of strong to severe thunderstorms which produced damaging to destructive winds across the northern and central Kansas. The Salina area had extensive tree damage and power outages from thunderstorm wind gusts over 80mph with a measurement of 95 mph in downtown.

Storms tracked out of northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska during the early evening hours of Wednesday July 3rd and moved into central Kansas around 11 pm. The line of storms accelerated southeast and produced a large swath of damaging winds as they tracked southeast through the overnight hours.||There were multiple reports of 70 mph winds with the Salina Airport recording an 82 mph wind gust with a mesonet in McPherson county reporting a 76 mph gust. Unfortunately there were 3 injuries and one fatality on Wilson Lake in Russell county when a number of campers were rolled by the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in the Oklahoma panhandle around 3 pm and tracked slowly east with time through the mid evening, even regenerating new cells.  Between 10 and 11 PM. The last of the multicell storms spread a little farther north along he Kansas and Oklahoma line. The airport AWOS at Elkhart reported a 72 mph wind gust around 10:20 PM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured at the Ekhart airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous multicell clusters developed near I-70 and spread east-southeast between about 5 pm and 10 pm, generally across the Ellis, Rush, Trego, Ness, Pawnee, Stafford and Pratt counties.   One inch diameter hail was measured by the public near Trego Center, but the vast majority of the area received heavy rain and there was an overall absence of severe criteria reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions in eastern Kansas improved significantly with no areas of severe drought (D2) remaining.  In central and parts of south central Kansas, drought conditions worsened with much of central Kansas now in the severe drought (D2) category.  This was due to the vast majority of the rain falling in southeast Kansas during the month of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened across the county from moderate drought (D1) to severe drought (D2).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours, scattered storms developed along the dryline as it moved over Northwest Kansas. One supercell produced an EF-Unknown tornado southeast of Atwood in Rawlins County The Rawlins County Storm and numerous others throughout the remainder of the afternoon produced pea to ping pong ball sized hail across Rawlins, Decatur, Thomas, Sheridan, Logan, and Gove counties. The largest hail fell near the town of Herndon in Rawlins County. The storms also produced damaging wind gusts of 58 to estimated 90 mph with damage to trees and a few overturned semi trucks across Logan, Gove, and Thomas counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to ping pong ball sized hail fell over the location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into central Kansas during the afternoon hours of April 30th and storms erupted along it by around 5 pm. Extreme instability allowed storms to quickly become severe producing large hail and a landspout tornado.  Storms continued to intensify as they tracked south and east with one of the storms producing up to baseball sized hail in Harper County! In addition, before moving into Oklahoma, one storm started to show impressive low level rotation and eventually produced a brief EF-1 tornado north of Sedan. One brief EF-U tornado occurred near Augusta.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report was received via mPing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From around 5:30 pm through the late evening, thunderstorms moved across an area of the westernmost counties in sw Kansas, with primarily strong but sub severe criteria wind gusts of up to 55 mph. One severe criterion wind gust of 62 mph was recorded at the HAYS airport around 11 pm.  Isolated hail was also occurring, evidenced by the public report of penny sized hail near Friend, KS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from Hays Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around 7 pm, discreet thunderstorms were moving south of Interstate 70 into the smoky hill region, while a more widespread severe line of storms was moving out of Colorado into the far southwest counties. Severe Thunderstorm Watches (#550 and 551) were in effect for the area as the line developed a large bow echo moving east across of most of the area south of highway 400. Numerous severe wind gusts were reported or recorded by spotters and mesonets, including two half dollar size hail reports in the Ford and Spearville areas of Ford county from a supercell that formed ahead of the convective line.

A complex of severe thunderstorms moved across the area, producing scattered wind damage across central and north-central Kansas, followed by a more concentrated area of 65-80 mph winds from Topeka to Lawrence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated 70 mph winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning around 7 pm, severe Thunderstorms spread into the area with a mix of impacts ranging from quarter to ping pong ball sized hail and severe wind gusts, reported as high as 70 mph, as was reported by the Garden City Airport observation at 9:20 pm. A torrential rain environment was also in place, as street flooding quickly developed in the town of Scott City. The severe weather continued into the overnight and early morning hours of July 7tt,  as 67 mph winds were measured by the mesonet station near Copeland around 2 AM. A prolific outflow boundary developed, moving across Gray county. A Damage survey conducted yielded estimated 100 mph wind gusts 5 miles southwest of Charleston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported by Emergency Management.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms tracked out of northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska during the early evening hours of Wednesday July 3rd and moved into central Kansas around 11 pm. The line of storms accelerated southeast and produced a large swath of damaging winds as they tracked southeast through the overnight hours.||There were multiple reports of 70 mph winds with the Salina Airport recording an 82 mph wind gust with a mesonet in McPherson county reporting a 76 mph gust. Unfortunately there were 3 injuries and one fatality on Wilson Lake in Russell county when a number of campers were rolled by the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple campers were blown over throughout the state park at Wilson Lake with debris spreading all over. Three people were transported to the hospital with injuries, and one person was found deceased.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front pushed south across the area on Tuesday, June 4th and was accompanied by a weak mid level disturbance. Storms developed during the early afternoon hours across west-central Kansas and tracked east into the late afternoon. As they tracked east they expanded in coverage and started to track southeast. ||The strongest storms produced larger than golf ball size hail across central Kansas along with 60 to 70 mph winds as they tracked southeast. In addition, due to the high moisture content of the airmass, they also produced very high rainfall rates which resulted in pockets of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report received via mPing.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Considerable flooding was reported in Fort Scott with many roads closed off. The Emergency Manager said he had not seen flooding as bad in over 35 years.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front pushed south across the area on Tuesday, June 4th and was accompanied by a weak mid level disturbance. Storms developed during the early afternoon hours across west-central Kansas and tracked east into the late afternoon. As they tracked east they expanded in coverage and started to track southeast. ||The strongest storms produced larger than golf ball size hail across central Kansas along with 60 to 70 mph winds as they tracked southeast. In addition, due to the high moisture content of the airmass, they also produced very high rainfall rates which resulted in pockets of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty-four hour rainfall of 1.89 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper low/trough was over the Central Plains which brought an increase in atmospheric lift. Moisture and instability increased across the area during the afternoon of the previous day. Wind shear and helicity increased after sunset. As the low moved northeast over Kansas and Nebraska, a cold front with gusty northwest winds moved into the area during the day on April 16th. These conditions were conducive to the development of severe weather. Strong to severe storms in the form of a QLCS moved northeastward across north central Kansas during the early morning hours of April 16th. The storms produced wind gusts up to 64 mph which resulted in wind damage, an EF-0 tornado (in Smith County), and a non-thunderstorm wind gust of 68 mph. The severe reports began just before 1 AM and ended just after 3 AM CDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located four miles north-northeast of Webster State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper low approached the Central Plains from the west, a dryline associated with it moved across the Southern Plains and portions of the Central Plains.  The surface pressure gradient strengthened just ahead of the upper low and resulted in strong winds across the Central Plains, including north central Kansas. Reports of high winds began during the early afternoon of April 6th and continued across the area into the late morning hours of April 7th. The highest wind gust was 71 mph measured by a mesonet station five miles west southwest of Agra in Phillips County. There was damage reported to power poles in the Stockton area in Rooks County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located in Plainville, and gusts of 62 MPH and 58 MPH were measured by a mesonet located four miles north-northeast of Webster State Park. Law enforcement reported damage to power poles in the Stockton area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 71 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located five miles west-southwest of Agra, a gust of 65 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located four miles east-southeast of Long Island, and a gust of 61 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located six miles north of Agra.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level trough from the west was beginning to move over the Central Plains, a surface low was centered over southern Kansas and northwest Oklahoma and extended northward into far southern Nebraska. A warm front associated with the surface low extended northward over northern Kansas. Winds were out of the northeast to the north of the warm front. Wind shear values of over 50 knots were present in north central Kansas with values decreasing into south central Nebraska. CAPE values of around 1,000 J/kg were across portions of north central Kansas and values near 500 J/kg were across portions of far south central Nebraska. Showers and thunderstorms had developed earlier in the day across north central Kansas and south central Nebraska. Another area of thunderstorms was in northwest Kansas and moved east northeast during the evening hours. This area of storms strengthened and moved into Phillips County and produced quarter size hail around 9:30 PM CDT.

A weak cold front pushed south across the area on Tuesday, June 4th and was accompanied by a weak mid level disturbance. Storms developed during the early afternoon hours across west-central Kansas and tracked east into the late afternoon. As they tracked east they expanded in coverage and started to track southeast. ||The strongest storms produced larger than golf ball size hail across central Kansas along with 60 to 70 mph winds as they tracked southeast. In addition, due to the high moisture content of the airmass, they also produced very high rainfall rates which resulted in pockets of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mostly smaller pea sized hail but a few golf ball sized hail pieces noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the heels of a promising winter 2023-24 season that brought above normal precipitation and drought improvement to this six-county North Central Kansas area, spring 2024 slowed the positive momentum. Following a March with at least slightly below normal precipitation across at least a slight majority of the area, April featured widespread below normal precipitation across the vast majority of the domain (see below for more detailed monthly precipitation info). As a result, for the first time in nearly a year, a calendar month featured greater areal coverage of drought degradation than improvement (per weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor/USDM). As a result, all six counties featured varying coverage of at least Moderate Drought (D1) during April, while five of the six counties held onto (or saw expansion of) D2 Severe Drought. April actually marked the 27th-consecutive month with at least D2 drought plaguing parts of the area. Focusing on April county-level USDM drought specifics, the month opened with roughly 76 percent of the six-county area assigned categories ranging from D0 (Abnormally Dry) to D2, with a more detailed breakdown as follows: 1) A few splotches of D2 nicked at least small portions of five of the six counties (except Phillips), but only constituted around 4% of the total domain...2) D1 comprised roughly 37% of North Central Kansas  (including sizeable portions of Jewell/Mitchell/Rooks counties)...3) D0 resided across roughly 35% of the area (including the majority of Rooks and Osborne counties)...4) the remaining approximately 24% of the area featured drought-free D-nothing (mainly within Smith/northern Phillips counties). Over the course of the month, most of North Central Kansas actually maintained status quo (including all of Phillips/Rooks and most of Smith counties). However, due to the prevailing dryness, decent portions of especially Mitchell/Osborne counties saw one-category degradation, with some places going from D1 to D2, and others going from D0 to D1. On one small positive note, a weather system late in the month on the 25th-26th brought beneficial rainfall of at least 1-3 to parts of especially Jewell and far northeastern Mitchell counties, prompting a few small pockets of one-category improvement. As a result of these changes, the end-of-April drought breakdown stood as follows: 1) coverage of D2 increased to now include roughly 12% of North Central Kansas (including roughly half of Mitchell County)...2) although it now enveloped more of especially Osborne County, the coverage of D1 across the six counties as a whole remained similar at roughly 37%...3) coverage of D0 decreased to around 27% of the area (mainly due to D1 taking over more of Osborne County)...4) the area of D-nothing focused primarily within Smith/northern Phillips counties remained unchanged, still constituting around 24% of the domain. ||Turning to April 2024 precipitation details, there were overall few positives to be found. Putting numbers to it, not only did around 90% of the six-county area register below normal precipitation (meaning less than roughly 2.31), but roughly half of North Central Kansas measured less than half its April normal (especially portions of Phillips/Rooks/Osborne/Mitchell counties). The only localized pockets of slightly above normal precipitation were mainly found within a narrow, west-east swath across central Jewell County. Per around 30 NWS/CoCoRaHs observers along with a few Kansas Mesonet stations, some of the paltriest April totals included: 0.44 six miles east of Phillipsburg, 0.49 at Webster Lake Dam, 0.56 five miles north of Plainville, and 0.65 in Cawker City. Meanwhile, a few of the only known very-near to above normal totals featured: 4.13 in Formoso, 3,04 near Burr Oak and 2.36 in Lebanon. On one final note, and as a testament to the magnitude of lingering/building longer-term drought: the NWS Cooperative Observer in Beloit totaled only 63.95 over the 37-month stretch from April 2021-April 2024...25.89 BELOW normal/71% of normal.

Over the course of the month, most of North Central Kansas actually maintained status quo (including all of Phillips/Rooks and most of Smith counties). However, due to the prevailing dryness, decent portions of especially Mitchell/Osborne counties saw one-category degradation, with some places going from D1 to D2, and others going from D0 to D1. On one small positive note, a weather system late in the month on the 25th-26th brought beneficial rainfall of at least 1-3 to parts of especially Jewell and far northeastern Mitchell counties, prompting a few small pockets of one-category improvement. As a result of these changes, the end-of-April drought breakdown stood as follows: 1) coverage of D2 increased to now include roughly 12% of North Central Kansas (including roughly half of Mitchell County)...2) although it now enveloped more of especially Osborne County, the coverage of D1 across the six counties as a whole remained similar at roughly 37%...3) coverage of D0 decreased to around 27% of the area (mainly due to D1 taking over more of Osborne County)...4) the area of D-nothing focused primarily within Smith/northern Phillips counties remained unchanged, still constituting around 24% of the domain. ||Turning to April 2024 precipitation details, there were overall few positives to be found. Putting numbers to it, not only did around 90% of the six-county area register below normal precipitation (meaning less than roughly 2.31), but roughly half of North Central Kansas measured less than half its April normal (especially portions of Phillips/Rooks/Osborne/Mitchell counties). The only localized pockets of slightly above normal precipitation were mainly found within a narrow, west-east swath across central Jewell County. Per around 30 NWS/CoCoRaHs observers along with a few Kansas Mesonet stations, some of the paltriest April totals included: 0.44 six miles east of Phillipsburg, 0.49 at Webster Lake Dam, 0.56 five miles north of Plainville, and 0.65 in Cawker City. Meanwhile, a few of the only known very-near to above normal totals featured: 4.13 in Formoso, 3,04 near Burr Oak and 2.36 in Lebanon. On one final note, and as a testament to the magnitude of lingering/building longer-term drought: the NWS Cooperative Observer in Beloit totaled only 63.95 over the 37-month stretch from April 2021-April 2024...25.89 BELOW normal/71% of normal.
Late in the evening on May 26th, storms on the backside of a surface low produced severe hail to Jarbolo in Leavenworth County and Olathe in Johnson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball sized hail was reported on the northwest side of Olathe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper low/trough was over the Central Plains which brought an increase in atmospheric lift. Moisture and instability increased across the area during the afternoon of the previous day. Wind shear and helicity increased after sunset. As the low moved northeast over Kansas and Nebraska, a cold front with gusty northwest winds moved into the area during the day on April 16th. These conditions were conducive to the development of severe weather. Strong to severe storms in the form of a QLCS moved northeastward across north central Kansas during the early morning hours of April 16th. The storms produced wind gusts up to 64 mph which resulted in wind damage, an EF-0 tornado (in Smith County), and a non-thunderstorm wind gust of 68 mph. The severe reports began just before 1 AM and ended just after 3 AM CDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado moved across rural areas of northern Smith County north of Athol. This tornado damaged some trees near its starting point and turned over a small mobile building along its way north. The tornado passed near a local limestone quarry. The path was based on a combination of damage indicators in the field and radar Correlation Coefficient signatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front pushed south across the area on Tuesday, June 4th and was accompanied by a weak mid level disturbance. Storms developed during the early afternoon hours across west-central Kansas and tracked east into the late afternoon. As they tracked east they expanded in coverage and started to track southeast. ||The strongest storms produced larger than golf ball size hail across central Kansas along with 60 to 70 mph winds as they tracked southeast. In addition, due to the high moisture content of the airmass, they also produced very high rainfall rates which resulted in pockets of flooding.
